1.1.1

Economics as a Social Science

Economics studies how individuals, firms, and governments make choices about scarce resources — and it does so using the methods of a social science, meaning it observes human behaviour, builds testable models, and gathers evidence, in a manner comparable to (though not identical to) the natural sciences.

Economics
The social science that studies how individuals, firms, and societies choose to allocate scarce resources among competing uses, in order to satisfy unlimited wants.
Social Science
A field of study that applies systematic, evidence-based methods to understand human behaviour and society — distinct from a natural science because it studies people, who can react to being studied, hold beliefs, and change their behaviour, making controlled experiments far harder to run.
FeatureNatural Sciences (e.g. Physics)Economics
Subject matterPhysical matter, obeying fixed natural laws.Human decision-makers, who can change their behaviour in response to new information or incentives.
Controlled experimentsUsually possible in a laboratory setting.Rarely possible on a whole economy — economists mostly rely on real-world data, statistical analysis, and models instead.
PredictionsTypically precise and highly reliable.Usually expressed as tendencies or probabilities ("a rise in price is likely to reduce quantity demanded"), rather than exact certainties, since human behaviour is harder to predict than physical matter.
Why Economics Still Counts as a Science: despite these differences, economics follows the same basic scientific method as the natural sciences — building simplified models of reality (companion methodology material below), deriving testable predictions from them, and revising or rejecting models that are contradicted by real-world evidence. This is precisely why economics is classified as a social science, rather than simply a branch of opinion or politics.
1.1.2

Positive and Normative Statements

A foundational distinction in economic methodology is between positive statements, which can be tested against evidence, and normative statements, which express a value judgement about what ought to happen.

Positive Statement
A statement of fact that can, in principle, be tested and shown to be true or false by reference to evidence — it describes "what is," not "what should be."
Normative Statement
A statement that expresses an opinion or value judgement about what is desirable — it cannot be proven true or false by evidence alone, since it depends on the values of the person making the judgement.
Positive Statement (Testable)Normative Statement (Value Judgement)
UK inflation was 3.2% in the year to a given month.The government should prioritise reducing inflation over reducing unemployment.
Raising the National Living Wage increased pay for the lowest-paid workers.The National Living Wage should be raised further, even if it costs some jobs.
A minimum unit price for alcohol reduced alcohol-related hospital admissions in Scotland.The government has a moral duty to reduce alcohol consumption through price controls.
Why This Distinction Matters Throughout the Course: almost every economic policy debate blends both types of statement — a positive claim about what a policy is likely to do, and a normative claim about whether that outcome is desirable. A well-structured exam answer should be able to identify which type of claim it is making at each stage of an argument, and should recognise that reasonable people can agree entirely on the positive economics of a policy while still disagreeing sharply about its normative desirability, because they hold different underlying values.
1.1.3

The Economic Problem

The economic problem is the starting point for the entire subject: because resources are scarce but human wants are unlimited, every individual, firm, and society is forced to make choices, and every choice carries a cost.

Scarcity
The fundamental economic condition that resources (land, labour, capital, enterprise) are finite, while human wants for goods and services are effectively unlimited — scarcity is what makes economic choice necessary in the first place.
Factors of Production
The four categories of resource used to produce goods and services: land (natural resources), labour (human effort), capital (man-made aids to production such as machinery), and enterprise (the risk-taking, organisational function of entrepreneurs).
Opportunity Cost
The value of the next best alternative forgone when a choice is made — because resources are scarce, choosing to use them one way always means giving up the benefit they could have provided in their next best alternative use.
1
Resources are scarce, but wants are unlimited

No society has ever had enough land, labour, capital, and enterprise to produce everything everyone might want.

2
Scarcity forces choice

Because not everything can be produced, a choice must be made about what to produce, how to produce it, and for whom.

3
Every choice carries an opportunity cost

Choosing to use scarce resources one way means forgoing whatever else those same resources could have produced — the true cost of any choice is best measured by what has been given up, not simply by its monetary price.

★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: NHS Funding Choices
Because the NHS budget is finite (scarcity), choosing to spend an additional £1 billion on cancer treatment has a genuine opportunity cost — that same £1 billion could instead have funded mental health services, additional GP appointments, or hospital maintenance backlogs. The economic problem does not disappear simply because healthcare feels like a moral necessity; scarcity still forces a choice about which valuable use of resources must be forgone.
1.1.4

Production Possibility Frontiers

A production possibility frontier (PPF) is the standard diagrammatic tool for illustrating the economic problem, opportunity cost, and the effects of economic growth, all in a single model.

Production Possibility Frontier (PPF)
A curve showing the maximum possible combinations of two goods (or categories of goods) that an economy can produce in a given time period, using all its available resources at maximum efficiency and with a given state of technology.
Fig 1: A production possibility frontier between capital goods and consumer goods, showing all three key points at once. C sits exactly on the frontier — productively efficient, using all available resources. D sits inside the frontier, representing underutilised resources (an economy that could produce more of both goods simply by using existing resources more fully). E sits outside the frontier and is currently unobtainable, however resources are reallocated, unless the whole PPF shifts outward through economic growth.
Cause of an Outward PPF Shift (Economic Growth)Explanation
An increase in the quantity of factors of productionFor example, net inward migration increasing the labour force, or new investment increasing the capital stock.
An improvement in the quality/productivity of factors of productionFor example, improved education and training raising labour productivity, or technological progress raising capital productivity.
Reading the Trade-Off Along the Curve: the PPF's concave (bowed-outward) shape reflects the fact that resources are not perfectly suited to producing every good equally well — as more resources are shifted from capital goods towards consumer goods, the first resources moved tend to be relatively better suited to consumer goods, but later resources are increasingly less well suited, so each further unit of consumer goods costs progressively more capital goods given up. This increasing opportunity cost is why the PPF curves rather than forming a straight line.
1.1.5

Specialisation and the Division of Labour

Specialisation occurs when individuals, firms, or countries concentrate on producing a narrow range of goods and services in which they are relatively more efficient, rather than each trying to produce everything they need themselves.

Specialisation
The concentration by an individual, firm, region, or country on producing a narrow range of goods or performing a narrow range of tasks, rather than attempting to be self-sufficient.
Division of Labour
A specific form of specialisation within a single production process, in which production is broken down into a sequence of separate, specialised tasks performed by different workers.
★ CLASSIC EXAMPLE: Adam Smith's Pin Factory
Adam Smith observed that a single untrained worker, performing every stage of pin-making alone, might produce only a handful of pins in a day. By breaking the process into around eighteen distinct specialised operations, a small factory of workers could collectively produce many thousands of pins per day — illustrating the enormous productivity gains available from dividing a production process into specialised tasks, even without any change in workers' individual skill or effort.
Benefit of SpecialisationExplanation
Higher productivityWorkers repeating a single task become faster and more skilled at it than a generalist attempting every task.
Use of comparative advantageEach worker, firm, or country can focus on the task or good it is relatively best suited to producing, raising total output for a given quantity of resources.
Encourages investment and innovationFocusing on one narrow task makes it worthwhile to invest in specialised machinery and techniques suited specifically to that task.
The Role of Money as a Medium of Exchange: specialisation only delivers its benefits if specialised producers can then exchange their output for the other goods and services they need — this makes an efficient means of exchange, historically and still today provided chiefly by money, a necessary companion to specialisation. Without money, exchange would require a "double coincidence of wants" (each party wanting exactly what the other has to offer), making direct barter an impractical basis for a highly specialised economy.
Potential Drawback of SpecialisationExplanation
Monotony and reduced job satisfactionHighly repetitive, narrow tasks can reduce worker morale and motivation compared with more varied work.
Structural unemployment riskWorkers who have specialised heavily in a single narrow skill can find it harder to find alternative work if demand for that specific skill declines.
Quality control riskA fault at one single specialised stage of a long, interdependent production process can disrupt the entire chain.
1.1.6

Free Market, Mixed and Command Economies

Every economy must resolve the same three fundamental questions raised by the economic problem — what to produce, how to produce it, and for whom — and different economic systems resolve them in fundamentally different ways.

Free Market Economy
An economic system in which resource allocation decisions are made almost entirely through the price mechanism, based on the independent decisions of private individuals and firms, with minimal government intervention.
Command (Planned) Economy
An economic system in which resource allocation decisions are made centrally by the government, which directly controls what is produced, how it is produced, and how it is distributed, rather than relying on market prices.
Mixed Economy
An economic system that combines elements of both the free market and command systems — most resource allocation occurs through markets, but government intervenes significantly in specific areas, such as providing public services, redistributing income, and regulating markets.
SystemHow "What, How, For Whom" Is DecidedKey AdvantageKey Disadvantage
Free market economyEntirely by the price mechanism — consumer spending and firms' profit-seeking behaviour.Highly efficient at processing decentralised information and responding quickly to changing conditions.No guarantee that outcomes are equitable, and markets can fail to provide public goods or correctly account for externalities.
Command economyEntirely by central government planning.Can direct resources towards specific national priorities and reduce inequality by design.Lacks the price signals needed to allocate resources efficiently, historically associated with shortages, surpluses, and weak incentives for innovation.
Mixed economyMostly by the price mechanism, with government intervention in specific areas (public goods, welfare, regulation).Aims to combine the efficiency of markets with government correction of their worst failures.Requires difficult, contested judgements about exactly how much and where government should intervene.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: Where Real Economies Sit
Almost every real-world economy is a mixed economy positioned somewhere along the spectrum between the two theoretical extremes. The United States is often cited as sitting closer to the free-market end, with a comparatively larger role for private provision even in areas such as healthcare. The UK sits further towards the mixed end, with substantial state provision of healthcare (the NHS) and education alongside a predominantly market-based economy. North Korea remains one of the few economies still operating close to a pure command system, with the state directly controlling the vast majority of production decisions. China represents a distinctive case: a predominantly state-directed political system that has nonetheless introduced substantial market mechanisms into its economy since economic reforms began in 1978, illustrating that the free market/command distinction is about economic decision-making specifically, not simply about the political system in place.
Exam Application: when asked to compare economic systems, avoid treating "free market" and "command economy" as the only two real-world options — almost every economy examiners will ask about is a mixed economy, and the strongest answers focus on where a given economy sits on the spectrum and why, rather than forcing it into one of the two pure theoretical extremes.
1.2.1

Rational Decision Making

Traditional economic theory begins from the assumption that individuals are rational: each person acts consistently to maximise their own utility, using the information available to them at the time of the decision.

Rational Economic Agent
A decision-maker assumed to act with self-interest, in a consistent manner, to maximise their own net benefit (utility for a consumer, profit for a firm) subject to the constraints they face, such as a limited budget.
Marginal Analysis
Weighing up the additional benefit against the additional cost of a small change in behaviour (e.g. buying one more unit of a good) — rational decisions are made "at the margin," not by considering a choice as all-or-nothing.
1
A rational consumer weighs marginal benefit against marginal cost

Before buying one more unit of a good, a rational consumer asks whether the extra satisfaction it provides is worth its price.

2
Diminishing marginal utility means this extra satisfaction falls as consumption rises

Each further unit of a good tends to deliver less additional satisfaction than the unit before it.

3
A rational consumer therefore only buys further units if price falls to match this declining marginal benefit

This is the theoretical foundation for the downward-sloping demand curve examined in Section 8.

A Simplifying Assumption, Not a Perfect Description: the rational model is a deliberately simplified assumption that allows economists to make clear, testable predictions about how people respond to changing incentives — it is not presented as a perfectly accurate account of every real decision. Section 16 examines how real behaviour often departs from this model in systematic, predictable ways.
1.2.2

Demand

A demand curve shows the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity of that good consumers are willing and able to buy, holding all other influences on demand constant (ceteris paribus). Almost without exception, demand curves slope downward from left to right — a relationship known as the law of demand.

Demand
The quantity of a good or service that consumers are both willing and able to purchase at a given price, in a given time period — willingness alone is not enough; the consumer must also have the means to pay.
Law of Demand
As the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded of it falls, and as price falls, quantity demanded rises — producing a demand curve that slopes downward from left to right.
Fig 2: A downward-sloping demand curve. A fall in price from P₁ to P₂ causes a movement along the curve — an extension of demand from Q₁ to Q₂. A rise in price would cause the reverse: a contraction of demand.

Two distinct economic explanations underpin the law of demand — diminishing marginal utility is one; the other rests on two effects that occur whenever price changes.

Income Effect
When the price of a good falls, a consumer's real income (purchasing power) effectively rises, since their fixed money income now buys more — this makes them able to buy more of the good, increasing quantity demanded.
Substitution Effect
When the price of a good falls relative to substitute goods, the good becomes relatively cheaper and more attractive compared with its alternatives, causing consumers to switch towards it, increasing quantity demanded.
Diminishing Marginal Utility
Because each additional unit of a good delivers less extra satisfaction than the last, consumers are only willing to pay a lower price to acquire further units — providing the utility-theory justification for the same downward-sloping shape.
Why All Three Explanations Are Valid Together: Edexcel does not require you to choose only one explanation for the law of demand — the income effect, substitution effect, and diminishing marginal utility are complementary explanations that all point towards the same downward-sloping relationship, and the strongest exam answers reference more than one where space allows.

Movements Along vs Shifts of the Demand Curve

One of the most heavily tested distinctions in this topic is between a movement along the demand curve, caused only by a change in the good's own price, and a shift of the whole demand curve, caused by a change in any other factor affecting demand.

Extension of Demand
A movement along the demand curve caused by a fall in the good's own price, resulting in a rise in quantity demanded (Q₁→Q₂ in Fig. 2).
Contraction of Demand
A movement along the demand curve caused by a rise in the good's own price, resulting in a fall in quantity demanded.
Shift of the Demand Curve
A movement of the entire demand curve to a new position, caused by a change in any determinant of demand other than the good's own price.
Determinant (Non-Price)Effect of an IncreaseUK Example
IncomeDemand for normal goods rises (shift right); demand for inferior goods falls (shift left) — see Section 9.Rising real wages increase demand for restaurant meals out.
Price of substitutesA rise in a substitute's price increases demand for this good (shift right) — see Section 9.A rise in Netflix subscription prices can increase demand for Amazon Prime Video.
Price of complementsA rise in a complement's price decreases demand for this good (shift left) — see Section 9.A rise in petrol prices can reduce demand for cars.
Tastes and fashionA good becoming more fashionable or socially desirable shifts demand right.A celebrity endorsement boosting demand for a trainer brand.
Population and demographicsA larger or ageing population shifts demand for relevant goods right.An ageing UK population increasing demand for social care and mobility aids.
Advertising and brandingSuccessful advertising shifts demand right by changing consumer perception.A supermarket's Christmas advertising campaign boosting seasonal demand.
Expectations of future pricesExpecting prices to rise in future increases current demand (bringing purchases forward).Stockpiling ahead of an announced VAT rise.
Exam Trap to Avoid: never describe a change in the good's own price as "shifting the demand curve" — this is one of the most common errors at this level. A change in a good's own price always produces a movement along a fixed curve (extension or contraction); only a change in something else entirely produces a shift of the curve itself.
1.2.3

Price, Income and Cross Elasticities of Demand

Elasticity measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price, income, or the price of another good — not merely the direction of the change, but its size relative to the change that caused it.

Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)
The percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.
Income Elasticity of Demand (YED)
The percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in income.
Cross Elasticity of Demand (XED)
The percentage change in quantity demanded of Good A divided by the percentage change in price of Good B.

Price Elasticity of Demand: Three Worked Examples

Worked Example 1 — An Elastic Good (Restaurant Meals)

The price of a restaurant meal rises from £20 to £25. Weekly quantity demanded falls from 100 meals to 70 meals.

% change in price = (25 − 20) ÷ 20 × 100 = +25%
% change in quantity demanded = (70 − 100) ÷ 100 × 100 = −30%
PED = −30% ÷ 25% = −1.2

Key point: since |PED| > 1, this good is relatively elastic — restaurant meals are a discretionary luxury with many substitutes (cooking at home, other restaurants), so quantity demanded is highly sensitive to price.

Worked Example 2 — An Inelastic Good (Prescription Insulin)

The price of insulin rises by 10%. Quantity demanded falls by only 2%, since patients cannot easily reduce medically necessary consumption.

PED = −2% ÷ 10% = −0.2

Key point: since |PED| < 1, demand is relatively inelastic — a necessity with no real substitute barely responds to price at all.

Worked Example 3 — Unit Elastic Demand

The price of a good rises by 10%. Quantity demanded falls by exactly 10%.

PED = −10% ÷ 10% = −1.0 (unit elastic)

Key point: when PED = −1 exactly, the percentage change in quantity demanded exactly matches the percentage change in price — a special case explored below through its link to total revenue.

PED ValueDescriptionMeaning
PED = 0Perfectly inelasticQuantity demanded does not change at all, whatever happens to price.
0 > PED > −1Relatively inelasticQuantity demanded changes by a smaller percentage than price (Worked Example 2).
PED = −1Unit elasticQuantity demanded changes by exactly the same percentage as price (Worked Example 3).
PED < −1Relatively elasticQuantity demanded changes by a larger percentage than price (Worked Example 1).
PED = −∞Perfectly elasticConsumers will buy an unlimited quantity at one price only, and none above it.
Determinant of PEDTends Towards InelasticTends Towards Elastic
Availability of substitutesFew or no close substitutes (e.g. insulin)Many close substitutes (e.g. one brand of bottled water)
Necessity vs luxuryA necessity (e.g. basic groceries)A luxury that can be postponed (e.g. a foreign holiday)
Proportion of incomeSmall share of budget (e.g. salt)Large share of budget (e.g. a car)
Time periodThe short runThe long run, once alternatives can be found
Habit-forming/addictive goodsAddictive goods (e.g. tobacco)Non-habit-forming goods
★ UK EXAMPLE: Rail Season Tickets vs Off-Peak Leisure Travel
Commuter rail season tickets tend to have relatively inelastic demand, since many commuters have no practical alternative way of reaching work at the required time — a major reason UK rail fares have historically risen faster than inflation with limited loss of custom. Off-peak leisure rail travel is far more elastic, since leisure travellers can more easily switch to coach travel, driving, or simply not travelling.

PED and Total Revenue

Because PED measures how responsive quantity demanded is to price, it directly predicts how a price change affects a firm's total revenue (price × quantity) — one of the most exam-relevant applications of elasticity.

PEDEffect of a Price Rise on Total Revenue
Inelastic (0 to −1)Total revenue rises — the small percentage fall in quantity is outweighed by the larger percentage rise in price.
Unit elastic (−1)Total revenue is unchanged.
Elastic (beyond −1)Total revenue falls — the large percentage fall in quantity outweighs the smaller percentage rise in price.

Worked Example — Revenue Effect of a Price Rise (Inelastic Good)

Using Worked Example 2: the price of insulin rises from £20 to £22 per unit (+10%), and quantity demanded falls from 1,000 to 980 units per week (−2%).

Original TR = £20 × 1,000 = £20,000
New TR = £22 × 980 = £21,560
Change in TR = +£1,560 (a rise, since demand is inelastic)
Why Governments Target Inelastic Goods with Tax: because a price rise on an inelastic good raises total revenue rather than reducing it, governments frequently target inelastic goods such as tobacco, alcohol, and fuel with indirect taxes (Section 15) — the tax raises substantial revenue precisely because quantity demanded barely falls, though this same inelasticity limits the tax's effectiveness at actually reducing consumption.

Income Elasticity of Demand

Worked Example 1 — A Normal (Luxury) Good: Restaurant Meals

Average household income rises by 8%. Demand for restaurant meals rises by 12%.

YED = +12% ÷ +8% = +1.5

Key point: YED is positive and greater than 1 — demand rises faster than income, identifying restaurant meals as a normal, income-elastic (luxury) good.

Worked Example 2 — An Inferior Good: Bus Travel

Average household income rises by 10%. Demand for local bus travel falls by 5%, as commuters switch to running their own car.

YED = −5% ÷ +10% = −0.5

Key point: YED is negative — demand falls as income rises, identifying bus travel as an inferior good.

YED ValueCategoryExample
YED < 0Inferior goodValue-brand ready meals; bus travel (Worked Example 2)
0 < YED < 1Normal necessityBasic groceries; household energy
YED > 1Normal luxuryRestaurant meals (Worked Example 1); foreign holidays
Why YED Matters for the Economic Cycle: firms selling goods with high positive YED (luxuries) see sales boom during economic growth but suffer disproportionately in a recession, while firms selling inferior goods can actually see sales rise during a downturn as households trade down — directly relevant to business planning across the economic cycle.

Cross Elasticity of Demand

Worked Example 1 — Substitutes: Tea and Coffee

The price of tea rises by 10%. Demand for coffee rises by 4%, as some tea drinkers switch.

XED = +4% ÷ +10% = +0.4

Key point: a positive XED identifies tea and coffee as substitutes. The relatively small size (+0.4) suggests they are fairly weak or distant substitutes.

Worked Example 2 — Complements: Petrol and Cars

The price of petrol rises by 20%. Demand for cars falls by 6%, as running costs deter buyers.

XED = −6% ÷ +20% = −0.3

Key point: a negative XED identifies petrol and cars as complements — consumed together, so a price rise in one reduces demand for the other.

XED ValueRelationship
XED > 0Substitutes — the larger the value, the closer the substitutes.
XED = 0Unrelated goods.
XED < 0Complements — the larger the negative value, the stronger the complementary relationship.
Exam Tip: for both YED and XED, always identify the sign first (which classifies the good or relationship) before discussing the size (which measures how strongly) — this two-step structure is consistently rewarded in Edexcel data-response and essay questions on elasticity.
1.2.4

Supply

A supply curve shows the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity that firms are willing and able to supply to the market, holding all other influences on supply constant. Supply curves typically slope upward from left to right, a relationship known as the law of supply.

Supply
The quantity of a good or service that firms are both willing and able to offer for sale at a given price, in a given time period.
Law of Supply
As the price of a good rises, the quantity supplied of it rises, and as price falls, quantity supplied falls — producing a supply curve that slopes upward from left to right.
Fig 3: An upward-sloping supply curve. A rise in price from P₁ to P₂ causes a movement along the curve — an extension of supply from Q₁ to Q₂. A fall in price would cause a contraction of supply.
1
A higher price means higher potential profit per unit

Assuming costs of production stay constant, a rise in the price a firm can charge directly increases the profit margin available on each additional unit sold.

2
Higher potential profit provides the incentive to expand production

Rational, profit-maximising firms respond to this incentive by increasing output to capture the higher available profit.

3
This produces an upward-sloping supply curve

As price rises, the quantity firms are willing to supply also rises, generating the positive price–quantity relationship shown in Fig. 3.

The Incentive Function of Price: the law of supply exists because price acts as a direct signal and incentive to profit-motivated firms — a rational firm has no reason to expand output unless doing so is expected to be more profitable, and a price rise (holding costs constant) is precisely what makes expanded output more profitable.

Causes of Shifts in the Supply Curve

Just as with demand (Section 8), a change in the good's own price causes only a movement along a fixed supply curve. A shift of the entire supply curve is caused only by a change in a non-price determinant of supply.

Determinant (Non-Price)Effect of a Favourable ChangeUK Example
Costs of productionA fall in the cost of raw materials, wages, energy, or rent increases supply (shift right), since production becomes more profitable at every price.Falling global wheat prices reducing bakery input costs.
TechnologyAn improvement in production technology increases supply (shift right) by lowering costs per unit.Automation in car manufacturing lowering unit costs.
Indirect taxesAn increase in an indirect tax decreases supply (shift left) — see Section 15.Increases in fuel duty raising the effective cost of supplying petrol.
SubsidiesA government subsidy increases supply (shift right) — see Section 15.Renewable energy subsidies increasing the supply of wind and solar power.
Number of firms in the marketNew firms entering the market increase total market supply (shift right).New entrants into the UK meal-kit delivery market increasing total market supply.
Weather and natural conditionsParticularly relevant to agricultural markets — favourable weather increases supply (shift right).A good harvest increasing UK wheat supply.
Expectations of future pricesFirms expecting prices to fall in future may increase current supply to sell now rather than later.Farmers selling stored grain ahead of an expected price fall.
Exam Trap to Avoid: exactly as with demand, never describe a change in the good's own price as a cause of a supply curve "shifting" — this always produces a movement along the existing curve. Only a genuine change in costs, technology, taxation, subsidy, firm numbers, or a similar non-price factor produces a shift of the whole curve.
1.2.5

Elasticity of Supply

Price elasticity of supply (PES) measures the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price.

Price Elasticity of Supply (PES)
The percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price.

Worked Example

Price rises 10%, quantity supplied rises 25% → PES = +2.5 (elastic)
Price rises 10%, quantity supplied rises 2% → PES = +0.2 (inelastic)
Fig 4: The four benchmark supply curves. Perfectly elastic (horizontal) and perfectly inelastic (vertical) are the two extremes; between them, a flatter line indicates relatively elastic supply, and a steeper line indicates relatively inelastic supply.
Determinant of PESTends Towards InelasticTends Towards Elastic
Spare capacityOperating near full capacitySignificant spare capacity
Stock levelsLow stockHigh stock, easily released
Time periodShort runLong run
Ease of storagePerishable goodsDurable, storable goods
UK Example: housing supply is well known to be highly price inelastic in the short run — planning permission and construction time mean that even a substantial rise in house prices cannot be met with a rapid increase in the number of homes built.
1.2.6

Price Determination

The equilibrium price is determined by the interaction of market demand and market supply — the single price at which quantity demanded exactly equals quantity supplied.

Equilibrium
A state of balance in a market where quantity demanded exactly equals quantity supplied at the prevailing price, so there is no inherent tendency for price to change further.
Disequilibrium
Any state of a market in which quantity demanded does not equal quantity supplied at the prevailing price, whether above or below the equilibrium price.
Excess Demand (Shortage)
Occurs when price is set below equilibrium, so quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied — some willing buyers cannot obtain the good.
Excess Supply (Surplus)
Occurs when price is set above equilibrium, so quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded — firms are left with unsold stock.
Fig 5: Market equilibrium at the intersection of demand and supply, at price Pe and quantity Qe — the only price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.
1
Excess demand pushes price up

At any price below Pe, quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, and frustrated buyers bid the price up.

2
Excess supply pushes price down

At any price above Pe, quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded, and firms holding unsold stock cut prices.

3
Only Pe has no further tendency to change

At equilibrium, both buyers and sellers are satisfied with the quantity traded, closing the gap that drove adjustment.

Analysing Changes in Equilibrium Price

Because equilibrium price and quantity are determined by the intersection of demand and supply, any shift of either curve (Sections 8 and 10) changes where that intersection occurs.

ChangeEffect on Equilibrium PriceEffect on Equilibrium Quantity
Demand increases (shifts right)RisesRises
Demand decreases (shifts left)FallsFalls
Supply increases (shifts right)FallsRises
Supply decreases (shifts left)RisesFalls
★ UK EXAMPLE: Energy Prices Following Russia's Invasion of Ukraine (2022)
A sharp reduction in the supply of natural gas to European markets following the 2022 invasion shifted the market supply curve for gas sharply left — with demand relatively unchanged in the short run (energy has few substitutes and low PED), the new equilibrium settled at a substantially higher price with only a modest fall in quantity traded, exactly the "supply decreases" pattern above, and a major driver of the UK cost-of-living crisis that followed.
Exam Technique for Diagram Questions: always follow four steps in order: (1) identify precisely which curve is affected and why; (2) draw the shift in the correct direction; (3) identify the new equilibrium point; (4) state clearly what happens to both equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity.
1.2.7

The Price Mechanism

Price performs three functions that together allocate resources and coordinate the decisions of millions of independent buyers and sellers without any central authority.

Rationing Function
Price allocates a scarce good among competing buyers — only those willing and able to pay the market price receive it.
Incentive Function
A price change incentivises rational agents to change behaviour — a higher price incentivises firms to supply more and consumers to demand less.
Signalling Function
A price change conveys information about relative scarcity throughout the economy, allowing decentralised decision-making without central coordination.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Rationing via Price During Supply Shocks
Following the UK's early-2023 shortage of fresh salad vegetables (caused by poor harvests in southern Europe and North Africa), retailers who allowed prices to rise saw quantity demanded fall to match the reduced quantity available — price performed the rationing function automatically. Where some retailers instead imposed purchase limits per customer, they were substituting an administrative rationing mechanism for the price mechanism.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The Incentive Function — Electric Vehicle Adoption
Rising petrol prices alongside falling battery costs have altered the relative price incentive facing UK motorists, incentivising a growing share of buyers to switch towards electric vehicles. The same shifting incentives have encouraged manufacturers to redirect investment towards electric vehicle production — the incentive function operating on both sides of the market simultaneously.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The Signalling Function — Global Commodity Prices
A rise in the world price of lithium signals to mining companies worldwide that lithium has become more valuable relative to the cost of extracting it, without any single body needing to identify or communicate the underlying cause (e.g. rising global electric vehicle demand). Mining firms across many countries can each respond to this single price signal entirely independently, illustrating how the price mechanism coordinates decisions across a global market without central planning.
Fundamental QuestionHow the Price Mechanism Answers It
What to produce?Firms produce goods consumers are willing to pay a profitable price for — rising prices and profit signal where demand is strongest.
How to produce it?Firms choose the least-cost combination of factors, incentivised by relative factor prices.
For whom to produce it?The rationing function allocates output to those willing and able to pay — reflecting the existing distribution of income and wealth.
Assessing the "Impersonal" Nature of the Price Mechanism: the price mechanism allocates resources based purely on willingness and ability to pay, without any judgement about who most "deserves" a good. This impersonality can be viewed as a genuine strength — it allocates resources without prejudice or favouritism — but equally as a weakness, since it is entirely indifferent to genuine need, and can allocate a scarce good (e.g. a life-saving medicine) to a wealthy buyer who simply wants it, ahead of a poorer buyer who urgently needs it. Which framing is more persuasive is a value judgement, not something economics alone can settle — precisely why the market failure and government intervention material later in this theme matters so much.
1.2.8

Consumer and Producer Surplus

Consumer surplus and producer surplus together measure the total welfare gain a market generates for buyers and sellers — a central tool for evaluating the impact of price changes, taxes, subsidies, and market failure throughout this specification.

Consumer Surplus
The difference between what consumers are willing to pay for a good and what they actually pay — the extra value consumers gain from paying less than their maximum willingness to pay.
Producer Surplus
The difference between the price producers actually receive for a good and the minimum price they would have been willing to accept to supply it — the extra value producers gain from receiving more than their minimum acceptable price.
Fig 6: Consumer and producer surplus at equilibrium. Consumer surplus (green) is the area between the demand curve (D1) and the equilibrium price P1, up to Q1 — the value consumers gain from paying P1 rather than the higher prices they would have been willing to pay for earlier units. Producer surplus (orange) is the area between the equilibrium price P1 and the supply curve (S1), up to Q1 — the value producers gain from receiving P1 rather than the lower prices they would have accepted for earlier units.
Why This Matters Throughout the Theme: total welfare in a market is measured as consumer surplus plus producer surplus. This combined measure is the standard tool used to evaluate the impact of an indirect tax or subsidy (Section 15), and to identify and measure the welfare loss caused by market failure (Section 18) — any policy or market failure that reduces the combined area of consumer and producer surplus relative to the free-market equilibrium represents a loss of economic welfare.
1.2.9

Indirect Taxes and Subsidies

Indirect taxes and subsidies are price-based tools that shift the supply curve, directly altering market price and quantity, and the resulting split of consumer and producer surplus (Section 14).

Indirect Tax
A tax on spending, levied on a good or service, which raises the cost of supplying it and shifts the supply curve left (upward).
Subsidy
A payment from government to producers that lowers the cost of supplying a good, shifting the supply curve right (downward).
Specific Tax
A fixed amount of tax per unit sold, shown as a parallel upward shift of the supply curve.
Ad Valorem Tax
A tax calculated as a percentage of the price, shown as a supply curve shift that widens at higher prices, since a percentage tax adds a larger absolute amount at a higher price.
Tax Incidence
The division of the burden of an indirect tax between consumers (who pay a higher price) and producers (who receive a lower post-tax price) — the split depends on the relative price elasticity of demand and supply.
Fig 7: An indirect tax and its incidence. A specific tax shifts supply from S1=MC to S2=S1+Tax, reducing quantity from QE to Qt and raising price from PE to PC. The tax burden splits: consumers pay the gap between PC and PE (burden on consumers), while firms absorb the gap between PE and PF (burden on firms). The triangle A+B is the resulting welfare loss.
Fig 8: A subsidy shifts supply from S=PMC=SMC down to "S (after subsidy)", lowering the effective price paid and raising quantity to Qs, closer to the socially optimal quantity where SMB would otherwise cross the original supply curve.

Worked Example — Calculating Tax Incidence

A specific tax of £2 per unit is imposed on a good with equilibrium price £10 and quantity 1,000 units. After the tax, price rises to £11.50 and quantity falls to 800 units.

Burden on consumers = New price − Original price = £11.50 − £10 = £1.50 per unit
Burden on firms = Tax − Burden on consumers = £2.00 − £1.50 = £0.50 per unit
Total tax revenue = £2 × 800 = £1,600

Key point: consumers bear three-quarters of this tax (£1.50 of the £2.00), indicating relatively inelastic demand — exactly the pattern expected for a demerit good with few substitutes (Section 18).

PolicyEffect on SupplyEffect on Price & QuantityUK Example
Indirect taxShifts leftPrice rises, quantity fallsFuel duty; the Soft Drinks Industry Levy ("sugar tax"); tobacco duty.
SubsidyShifts rightPrice falls, quantity risesRenewable energy subsidies; subsidised childcare.
Tax Incidence Depends on Elasticity: where demand is inelastic, consumers bear most of the tax burden, which is precisely why demerit goods (Section 18) with inelastic demand are common targets for indirect taxation: revenue is raised with only a modest fall in the quantity consumed. Where demand is elastic, firms bear more of the burden, since raising price sharply would drive away too many customers.
1.2.10

Alternative Views of Consumer Behaviour

Behavioural economics draws on psychology to examine how real people actually make decisions, directly challenging the rational agent assumption introduced in Section 7.

Bounded Rationality
Individuals have limited time, information, and mental processing capacity, so they make decisions that are merely "good enough" rather than objectively optimal, often relying on simplified rules of thumb.
Bounded Self-Control
Individuals often know what would be in their own long-term best interest but lack the willpower to act on it consistently, over-valuing immediate gratification relative to future benefits.
Heuristics (Rules of Thumb)
Mental shortcuts used to make decisions quickly, which are often useful but can produce systematic, predictable errors — including anchoring (over-relying on the first piece of information encountered) and availability bias (judging probability by how easily examples come to mind).
Social Norms
Individuals are influenced by the perceived behaviour of others, even when this has no direct financial bearing on their own decision.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Bounded Rationality — Mobile Phone Contracts
Consumers choosing a mobile phone contract face a bewildering array of tariffs combining data, minutes, texts, and handset cost across dozens of providers. Rather than calculating the objectively cheapest option for their exact usage pattern, most consumers rely on heuristics — sticking with a familiar provider, picking a mid-priced tariff, or copying a friend's choice — precisely the "good enough" decision-making bounded rationality predicts, rather than the exhaustive optimisation the rational model assumes.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Bounded Self-Control — Gym Membership
Many consumers sign up for annual gym memberships fully intending to attend regularly, yet attendance data consistently shows a large share of members visit rarely after the first few weeks while continuing to pay — a direct illustration of bounded self-control, where good long-run intentions (fitness, health) are undermined by weak willpower in the moment (choosing the sofa over the gym), even though the rational, utility-maximising model would predict members either attend consistently or cancel.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The Behavioural Insights Team ("Nudge Unit")
The UK government established the Behavioural Insights Team in 2010 to apply behavioural economics findings to public policy. One well-known intervention reworded letters to owners of untaxed vehicles, adding a photograph of the specific untaxed car — a small design change that substantially increased payment rates by making the message feel personal, without altering the underlying fine at all.
Nudge
A gentle intervention in choice architecture that steers people towards a particular decision without forbidding any option or significantly changing its economic incentives.
Default Choice
The option received automatically unless a person actively opts out — exploiting the tendency to stick with whatever requires no action, as in UK workplace pension auto-enrolment.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Pension Auto-Enrolment
Before 2012, UK workers had to actively opt in to a workplace pension — take-up was low, particularly among younger and lower-paid workers, consistent with bounded rationality (the decision felt complex and easy to postpone) and bounded self-control (saving now for a distant retirement competes poorly against immediate spending). Since auto-enrolment made pension saving the default (with an opt-out available), participation rates rose sharply, even though the financial incentives to save were essentially unchanged — a striking demonstration that how a choice is presented can matter as much as its underlying economic terms.
Why This Matters for Policy Design: if real behaviour predictably departs from the rational model, purely incentive-based policy (taxes, fines) may be less effective than the rational model predicts, and policymakers increasingly design choice architecture — defaults, framing, nudges — to correct or work with these predictable departures, rather than relying on price-based incentives alone.
1.3.1

Types of Market Failure

Market failure occurs whenever the free market leads to a misallocation of resources relative to the socially optimal outcome — but not all market failure is equally severe.

Market Failure
A situation in which the free market mechanism results in an allocation of resources that does not maximise society's overall welfare.
Complete Market Failure (Missing Market)
A situation in which no market exists at all for a good, even though it is genuinely valued by society.
Partial Market Failure
A situation in which a market exists and functions, but at a price and/or quantity that misallocates resources relative to the social optimum.
TypeExampleWhy It Fits This Category
Complete market failureNational defence (Section 19)No private firm can profitably sell national defence to individual citizens, since it is impossible to exclude non-payers from its benefits — the market for it simply does not exist without government provision.
Complete market failureStreet lightingNo firm can charge individual pedestrians for the light they benefit from as they walk past — again, no market can form at all.
Partial market failureCigarettes (Section 18)A market for cigarettes clearly exists and functions, but because smokers do not bear the full social cost of their consumption, the market produces and consumes more than is socially optimal.
Partial market failurePrivate healthcare insuranceA functioning market exists, but asymmetric information (Section 20) between insurer and applicant can lead to under- or over-provision of cover relative to the social optimum.
Cause of Market FailureBrief DescriptionCovered In
ExternalitiesCosts or benefits affecting third parties, not reflected in the market price.Section 18
Public goodsNon-rival, non-excludable goods the free market fails to provide.Section 19
Information gapsBuyers and/or sellers lack the information needed to make rational decisions.Section 20
Why This Distinction Matters for Policy: complete market failure generally requires government to provide the good directly, since there is no market mechanism to correct — while partial market failure can often be addressed by correcting the price signal within an existing market (taxes, subsidies, regulation, Section 21) rather than replacing the market altogether. Confusing the two leads to proposing the wrong type of policy solution: taxing a good that doesn't exist in a market achieves nothing, and directly providing a good that merely needs a corrective tax wastes public resources on something the private sector could still supply.
1.3.2

Externalities

An externality exists whenever there is a divergence between the private costs or benefits faced by those directly involved in a transaction, and the full social costs or benefits experienced by society, including third parties.

Social Cost/Benefit
Private cost/benefit plus any external cost/benefit imposed on or enjoyed by third parties.
Externality
A cost or benefit falling on a third party not directly involved in a transaction, not reflected in the market price.
Fig 9: A negative production externality. Because the firm ignores the external cost (EC) it imposes on third parties, MSC lies above MPC. The market produces at the higher output where MPC meets demand — beyond the socially optimal output where MSC meets demand. The resulting gap is the welfare loss caused by over-production.
Fig 10: A negative consumption externality (e.g. cigarette smoking). MSB lies below MPB, since consumers overestimate their own true benefit and/or ignore the negative externality their consumption imposes on others (secondhand smoke, NHS treatment costs). The market over-consumes relative to the socially optimal quantity, creating a welfare loss — the consumption-side counterpart to the production-side externality in Fig. 9.
TypeRelationshipOutcomeUK Real-World Example
Negative production externalityMSC > MPCOver-productionIndustrial air pollution from a steel manufacturing plant.
Positive production externalityMSC < MPCUnder-productionBeekeeping, whose bees pollinate surrounding orchards free of charge.
Negative consumption externalityMSB < MPBOver-consumptionCigarette smoking, which generates secondhand smoke and NHS treatment costs.
Positive consumption externalityMSB > MPBUnder-consumptionEducation, which raises workforce productivity and reduces crime beyond the individual's own benefit.
Why the Absence of Property Rights Causes Externalities: externalities fundamentally arise because no one owns, or can enforce a price for, the affected third-party resource — no one owns "clean air," so a polluting factory faces no direct cost for degrading it. Where property rights can be clearly defined and enforced (e.g. tradeable pollution permits, Section 21), private markets can, in principle, resolve the externality without further government intervention.
A Note on Terminology: unlike some other exam boards, Edexcel does not use "merit good" and "demerit good" as separate named categories — content relating to goods like education, healthcare, tobacco, and alcohol is examined here directly as positive and negative externalities of consumption, combined with the information gaps examined in Section 20.
1.3.3

Public Goods

A pure public good is defined by two characteristics that, together, mean the free market will typically fail to provide it at all.

Non-Rivalry
One person's consumption of a good does not reduce the quantity or quality available for anyone else to consume.
Non-Excludability
It is impossible, or prohibitively costly, to prevent anyone from consuming the good once it has been provided, whether or not they have paid for it.
Free-Rider Problem
The tendency for individuals to avoid paying for a non-excludable good, since they can enjoy its benefits regardless of their own contribution — if enough people reason this way, insufficient revenue is raised to provide the good at all.
GoodRivalry?Excludability?Classification
A sandwichRivalExcludablePrivate good
National defenceNon-rivalNon-excludablePure public good
Street lightingNon-rivalNon-excludablePure public good
An uncongested roadNon-rival (until congested)Excludable via tollsQuasi-public good
Fig 11: The full classification of goods. Crossing excludability against rivalry produces four categories: private goods (excludable and rivalrous, e.g. food, clothes, cars); club goods (excludable but non-rivalrous, e.g. cinemas, private parks, satellite TV); common goods (non-excludable but rivalrous, e.g. fish, timber, coal); and pure public goods (non-excludable and non-rivalrous, e.g. air, national defence). Real goods often sit closer to one corner than another rather than fitting a category perfectly.
Fig 12: Underprovision of a public good due to the free-rider problem. MSB and MSC cross at Q*, the socially efficient quantity — society would clearly benefit from a positive quantity of the good being provided, since MSB exceeds MSC at every quantity below Q*. Yet because no individual can be excluded from the benefit once the good exists, the free-rider problem means the private market provides none of it at all: actual provision sits at Q=0, however large the gap between total social benefit and total social cost.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Technological Change and Excludability — Television Broadcasting
Early terrestrial television was effectively non-excludable: anyone with a receiver could pick up a broadcast signal, with no practical way to prevent non-payers watching. The development of encrypted satellite, cable, and digital streaming introduced genuine excludability, transforming television from a good with public-good characteristics into an almost fully excludable private good — illustrating that whether a good is excludable is not fixed, but can change with technology.
The Tragedy of the Commons: a related but distinct problem arises where a resource is non-excludable but rival — for example, fish stocks in international waters. Because no one owns the resource, no individual user has a private incentive to conserve it, leading to systematic overuse and depletion, as seen in the historic collapse of North Sea cod stocks. This model is central to understanding environmental market failures, from overfishing to the atmosphere's capacity to absorb carbon emissions.
1.3.4

Information Gaps

The price mechanism can only allocate resources efficiently if buyers and sellers have adequate information to make rational decisions. Where information is imperfect, or asymmetric between the two sides of a transaction, market failure results.

Imperfect Information
A situation in which buyers and/or sellers do not have all the relevant information needed to make a fully informed decision.
Asymmetric Information
A specific form of imperfect information in which one party to a transaction knows substantially more relevant information than the other party.
Moral Hazard
A situation arising after a transaction has taken place, in which one party changes their behaviour because they no longer bear the full consequences of their actions — for example, a person with comprehensive insurance taking less care to avoid a claim, since the insurer bears most of the resulting cost.
Adverse Selection
A situation arising before a transaction takes place, in which asymmetric information causes a disproportionate share of "bad" participants to take part in a market — for example, those most likely to claim being most likely to seek insurance, raising costs for insurers and, eventually, for all policyholders.
★ CLASSIC EXAMPLE: The Used Car Market ("Lemons" Problem)
A used car's seller typically knows far more about a vehicle's true condition than a prospective buyer, who cannot fully verify whether the car is reliable or riddled with hidden faults. Buyers, unable to distinguish good cars from bad, are only willing to pay an average price reflecting their uncertainty — which can push genuinely good cars out of the market altogether, a market failure caused entirely by the information gap rather than by any change in the cars themselves.
★ UK EXAMPLE: PPI Mis-Selling and Food Labelling
UK banks' mis-selling of Payment Protection Insurance is a well-documented case of asymmetric information: banks understood the low likelihood that many customers would ever successfully claim, information customers themselves typically lacked, leading to widespread purchase of a poorly suited product and, eventually, over £38 billion in compensation payouts. Consumers similarly often underestimate the sugar or salt content of processed foods marketed with health-conscious branding, a key reason for mandatory nutritional labelling.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Moral Hazard in Insurance and Banking
A driver with fully comprehensive car insurance may take marginally less care to avoid minor accidents than an uninsured driver would, since the financial consequences fall largely on the insurer rather than themselves — classic moral hazard. At a larger scale, banks widely regarded as "too big to fail" ahead of the 2008 financial crisis are argued to have taken excessive risks partly because they expected government to bear the cost of any resulting failure, a moral hazard problem at the heart of ongoing debates about bank regulation and bailouts.
Why Information Gaps Interact With Externalities: information gaps often compound the externality problems examined in Section 18 — a smoker who underestimates their own long-run health risk (information gap) over-consumes cigarettes for two distinct reasons simultaneously: the negative externality imposed on others, and the misjudged private cost to themselves. Both elements are separately assessable and should be identified explicitly in an exam answer.
1.4.1

Government Intervention in Markets

The existence of market failure (Sections 17–20) provides the economic case for government intervention. Government has a range of tools available, each suited to different circumstances.

Fig 13: A price floor (minimum price) set above the equilibrium price Pe. At the price floor Pf, quantity supplied Qs exceeds quantity demanded Qd, creating excess supply ("surplus") of unsold output. Consumer surplus shrinks (fewer units bought, at a higher price), while total welfare falls by the deadweight loss triangle — the value of trades between Qd and Qe that no longer take place because the price floor prevents the market clearing at Pe.
Fig 14: A price ceiling (maximum price) set below the equilibrium price Pe. At the price ceiling, quantity supplied QS falls short of quantity demanded QD, creating a shortage — some consumers willing to pay the capped price cannot obtain the good. Producer surplus shrinks sharply (region F), while the triangle C+E is a deadweight loss: the value of trades between QS and equilibrium that no longer take place because the price ceiling prevents suppliers from being paid enough to produce that quantity.
ToolBest Suited ToUK Example
Indirect taxesCorrecting negative externalities by raising private cost towards social cost (Section 15).Fuel duty; the Soft Drinks Industry Levy; tobacco duty.
SubsidiesCorrecting positive externalities by lowering private cost of provision (Section 15).Renewable energy subsidies; subsidised childcare.
Maximum price (price ceiling)Making an essential good more affordable, though risking excess demand if set below equilibrium.Energy price caps on the maximum unit price suppliers can charge.
Minimum price (price floor)Correcting under-pricing of a good relative to its true social cost, though risking excess supply.Minimum unit pricing for alcohol in Scotland and England.
Pollution permitsCorrecting the tragedy of the commons by creating tradeable property rights over a previously unowned resource.The UK Emissions Trading Scheme, capping and trading carbon emissions.
Provision of informationCorrecting the information gaps examined in Section 20 directly, without changing price or restricting choice.NHS anti-smoking campaigns; mandatory nutritional labelling.
RegulationRestricting a harmful activity or enforcing minimum standards where a price-based tool alone is judged insufficient.The UK ban on smoking in enclosed public places (2007); food hygiene standards.
State provision of public goodsPure public goods where the free-rider problem prevents any private market from emerging (Section 19).The NHS; state-funded education; national defence; flood defences.
Choosing Between Tools: the strongest evaluative answers explicitly compare tools for a specific market failure rather than simply listing them — for example, a good with inelastic demand may respond poorly to a tax (Section 15) but strongly to a minimum price targeting the very cheapest units, or to information provision addressing the underlying misjudgement directly.
1.4.2

Government Failure

Just as markets can fail, so too can government intervention itself lead to a misallocation of resources — meaning market failure alone does not automatically justify intervention.

Government Failure
A situation in which government intervention leads to a net misallocation of resources, potentially making the overall outcome worse than before the intervention.
Cause of Government FailureExplanation
Information gapsGovernment, like markets, may lack the precise information needed to correctly measure an externality or set an optimal tax or subsidy — an incorrectly calibrated intervention can over- or under-correct the original problem.
Opportunity and administrative costsDesigning, implementing, and enforcing any intervention consumes real resources that must be weighed against the intervention's benefits, and represent resources that could have been used elsewhere.
Price signal distortionsGovernment intervention can itself distort price signals, encouraging unintended behaviour that would not occur under a well-functioning free market.
Conflicting objectivesA government pursuing efficiency, equity, and political objectives simultaneously may design an intervention that serves one at the expense of another.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The EU Common Agricultural Policy
The EU's Common Agricultural Policy historically guaranteed minimum prices to European farmers well above the equilibrium market price, intended to support farm incomes. The unintended consequence was chronic over-production — infamously described as "butter mountains" and "wine lakes" — as farmers responded rationally to the guaranteed price by producing far more than consumers actually demanded, with the EU then forced to buy and store the surplus at further cost to taxpayers, a clear case of price signal distortion creating a new, costly problem.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Right to Buy and the Long-Run Reduction in Social Housing
The 1980 Right to Buy policy allowed council tenants to purchase their homes at a substantial discount, successfully expanding homeownership among many working-class households. An unintended long-run consequence was a significant reduction in the available stock of social housing, since replacement building did not keep pace with sales — an intervention aimed at one objective (expanding homeownership) creating a genuine cost elsewhere (reduced social housing supply) that policymakers did not fully anticipate.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The Universal Credit Five-Week Wait
Universal Credit was designed to simplify the UK benefits system and improve work incentives by replacing six separate means-tested benefits with a single payment. Its rollout revealed a significant unintended consequence: a built-in five-week wait for a first payment pushed some new claimants into debt or reliance on food banks before receiving any support — illustrating that even a well-evidenced policy response to a genuine problem (the old system's complexity) can generate a costly new problem of its own.
Why Government Failure Does Not Mean "Do Nothing": the existence of government failure does not imply government should never intervene — it means the case for any specific intervention must weigh the likely benefit of correcting the original market failure against the likely cost of the specific policy's own imperfections. Exam answers should end with a reasoned judgement on whether the market failure is worse than the risk of government failure in that specific context, rather than treating intervention as automatically beneficial.
Exam Prep

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: From Externality to Corrective Policy

A firm's production imposes a cost on third parties not reflected in its own private costs (Section 18) → MSC exceeds MPC, and the free market produces beyond the socially optimal quantity, creating a welfare loss measured in lost consumer and producer surplus (Section 14) → this over-production is a market failure, providing the case for government intervention (Section 21) → government imposes an indirect tax equal to the external cost, shifting supply left → output falls towards the socially optimal quantity, provided the tax is correctly calibrated → if the tax is mis-sized or firms respond in unanticipated ways, the correction may be incomplete or create a new problem — a risk of government failure (Section 22) that must be weighed against the market failure being addressed.

The Golden Chain: From Non-Excludability to State Provision

A good is non-rival and non-excludable (Section 19) → rational individuals have no private incentive to pay voluntarily, since they cannot be excluded from its benefits regardless (the free-rider problem) → insufficient revenue is raised through voluntary means, even though total social benefit may vastly exceed total cost → this is complete market failure — a missing market (Section 17) — meaning price-based correction cannot work, since there is no market to correct → government must instead fund provision directly through compulsory general taxation (Section 21).

Key Exam Tips

1

Always identify whether a scenario describes positive or normative economics (Section 2) before analysing it — this is frequently tested directly and underpins the evaluative "it depends on your values" conclusions Edexcel rewards.

2

For any elasticity calculation, show both percentage changes as separate steps before dividing (Section 9) — method marks are awarded for each stage even if the final answer contains an error.

3

Use consumer and producer surplus explicitly (Section 14) when evaluating the welfare impact of a tax, subsidy, price control, or market failure — Edexcel expects this analytical tool to be applied consistently across Theme 1, not only in the specific section it was introduced.

4

Remember Edexcel does not name "merit" and "demerit" goods separately (Section 18) — analyse these goods directly through externalities and information gaps instead.

5

For any externality diagram, label MPC/MSC or MPB/MSB explicitly, mark both Qm and Qopt, and shade the welfare loss triangle — these are the specific elements Edexcel examiners look for.

6

Always end government intervention questions with an explicit, reasoned judgement weighing the market failure against the risk of government failure (Section 22) — Edexcel's less prescriptive mark schemes reward the quality of this final reasoning highly, even if not every possible point has been covered.

BIZ-OMICS | biz-omics.co.uk | Edexcel A-Level Economics A | Theme 1: Introduction to Markets and Market Failure
